Lugano

TITicino

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The cherry trees of Ceresio

A child discovers why the lake was named after cherries and follows a trail of vanishing cherry blossoms to a secret grove where a Roman gardener's spirit still tends the ancient trees. Each blossom reveals a chapter of the lake's history, from its glacial birth to the days when cherry trees lined every shore.

Create This Story

Lake Lugano was first cited by Gregory of Tours in 590 AD with the name Ceresio — derived from the Latin cerasus ('cherry'), referring to the abundance of cherry trees that once adorned its shores. The lake covers 48.7 km², with 63% in Switzerland and 37% in Italy, and reaches a maximum depth of 288 metres. It was formed during the last ice age by retreating glaciers carving a deep valley. The dramatic, fjord-like geography distinguishes it from the gentler shores of Lake Como or Lake Maggiore.

Luini's 153 figures

A child visiting Santa Maria degli Angioli notices one figure in the fresco winking. Stepping into the painting, the child must find the way out by solving riddles hidden among the 153 characters — each painted figure holds a clue left by Luini himself, and only by understanding the master's art can the child return to the real world.

Create This Story

The Chiesa di Santa Maria degli Angioli houses the most important Renaissance fresco in Switzerland: the Passion and Crucifixion by Bernardino Luini (1480-1532), a pupil of Leonardo da Vinci. Dating to 1529, the fresco covers an entire wall and features approximately 153 figures — soldiers on horseback, crowds, and the three crosses at the centre. The church was built between 1499 and 1500 as part of a Franciscan monastery founded in 1490, located on Lugano's lakeside promenade.

The liberty tree of 1798

On Piazza della Riforma, a magical tree sprouts overnight. A child discovers it is the original liberty tree from 1798, and its roots hold the memory of the night Lugano chose to be free and Swiss. Each branch tells a different story of the town's fierce independence, from the medieval disputes between Como and Milan to the moment the citizens planted their flag in the square.

Create This Story

On 15 February 1798, Lugano residents stormed the town hall. Rather than join Napoleon's Cisalpine Republic, they erected a liberty tree on Piazza Grande (today Piazza della Riforma) and declared themselves 'liberi e svizzeri' ('free and Swiss'). The square was renamed Piazza della Riforma in 1830 to commemorate the reform of the cantonal Constitution. In 1803, the Act of Mediation unified the eight former bailiwicks into the Canton of Ticino.

The pilgrims of San Salvatore

A child rides the funicular up Monte San Salvatore and meets a ghostly medieval pilgrim from the year 1200 who has lost the way to the chapel at the summit. Together they climb through centuries of devotion, passing pilgrims from every era, and the child learns that some journeys are not about the destination but about the faith that keeps people climbing.

Create This Story

Monte San Salvatore (912 m), known as Switzerland's Sugar Loaf Mountain, towers over Lugano from the south. According to ancient legend, Christ himself stopped to rest on this summit during his Ascension to heaven. Since 1200, pilgrims have climbed the steep path from Paradiso to the summit. A chapel existed from the 13th century; the current church was built in 1722. The funicular railway from Paradiso has operated since 1890, climbing 600 metres in altitude.

Hermann Hesse's paintbox

In the Torre Camuzzi museum, a child touches one of Hesse's watercolours and is transported into its landscape — a dreamy, painted version of Montagnola where colours have feelings and every brushstroke tells a story. The child must find their way home through Hesse's painted world, where the hills glow amber and the lake shimmers in impossible blues.

Create This Story

Hermann Hesse (1877-1962) arrived in Montagnola above Lugano in 1919. He rented rooms in the Casa Camuzzi, a villa designed by Ticinese architects who had worked in 19th-century Saint Petersburg. In the peace of Ticino, Hesse rediscovered writing and took up watercolour painting. Major works written there include Siddhartha (1922) and Steppenwolf (1927). He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1946. The Museo Hermann Hesse, inaugurated in 1997 in the Torre Camuzzi, preserves tokens of his last 43 years as writer and painter.

The smuggler's submarine of Gandria

A child exploring the Customs Museum discovers the miniature submarine is actually real-sized at midnight. An adventure across Lake Lugano follows, dodging border guards and uncovering the smuggler's hidden cargo in a moonlit chase between Switzerland and Italy on the mysterious waters of the Ceresio.

Create This Story

Gandria, first documented in 1237, was originally only accessible by boat and steep trails. Across the lake at Cantine di Gandria stands the Swiss Customs Museum, housed in a former border post accessible only by boat. The museum tells the story of smuggling on the Swiss-Italian border: in November 1948, Italian border guards discovered a 3-metre-long submarine on the lake bank, capable of transporting 450 kg of contraband. A road finally connected Gandria to Lugano and Italy only in 1935.

The secret press of Capolago

A child finds a hidden printing press in a cellar near the lake. The type-blocks come alive, spelling out messages from the Risorgimento exiles who once printed forbidden books for Italian freedom. Each letter pressed onto paper reveals a different exile's story — of sacrifice, courage, and the belief that words can change the world.

Create This Story

The Tipografia Elvetica in nearby Capolago (1830-1853), founded by Genoese exile Alessandro Repetti, was the most important clandestine press of the Italian Risorgimento — printing works by Mazzini, Cattaneo, Gioberti, and d'Azeglio, smuggled across the border into Italy. Collaborator Luigi Dottesio was executed by the Austrians in 1851. Carlo Cattaneo, leader of the Five Days of Milan (March 1848), lived in exile in Castagnola near Lugano from 1848, serving as professor of philosophy at the Lyceum of Lugano until his death in 1869.

Swissminiatur after dark

When the park closes, the 130 tiny buildings come alive at 1:25 scale. A child shrunk to miniature size must ride the tiny trains across all of Switzerland — from the castles of Bellinzona to the Federal Palace in Bern — to find the way home before sunrise, discovering the entire country one miniature landmark at a time.

Create This Story

Swissminiatur, opened in 1959 in Melide, is the only miniature park in Switzerland, covering 14,000 m² with over 130 models at 1:25 scale of Switzerland's most famous buildings and monuments. It features 3,560 metres of miniature railway with 18 trains, plus boats, funiculars, and cars. Notable models include the castles of Bellinzona, the Federal Palace in Bern, and Piazza Grande in Locarno.

Upload a photo
